In fact, I would say that when I was very young, my first job as a real sales person, right? I was always selling a, knocked on doors with, newspaper route when I was eight, shoveling driveways when I was 10. And yeah, you can make sure that you look back, you'd see me with that big smile and sort of sympathetic look, you know, when I go, it's like, you know, the kid in distress, would you please sign up for my paper route because my mommy's not gonna feed me otherwise? Like, you know, it's like, I had this sort of natural ability to connect with people the second they laid eyes on me, big smile, bright old good eye contact and so forth. But the first time that I really went out into the world to sell, to close, was when I was 21 years old and I was going door to door, selling meat and seafood, right? And you know, you probably know the story, but you know, I answered this blind-ended newspaper and I walked in there and they were selling meat, steaks, flaming yon, shrimp, lobsters, out of the back of these Toyota pickup trucks, they had these wooden boxes with dry ice on top. And it was all door to door or business to business, you walk in there cold-colding, selling them boxes of frozen steaks and shrimps for their freezers, right? Like freezer plans, right? So the training was such that, you know, you just simply would sign up and they'd have you sit in a truck and just watch someone, you know, go door to door for a day, right? And that was how you learned to go. And I did that, and I'll tell you that story because it's some great by-language stuff. But my first day on the job when I got out there and that first door I knocked on, right? That first woman who answered, I ended up selling a 13 boxes of meat. It was like, she's probably still eating it 30 years later, right? 13 boxes of meat, right? Which was massive called the baker's dozen, right? And I still remember to this day, knowing that when she opened that big smile, that eye contact, the way I engaged her, I knew how important it was. It just so something, that's how my brain was wired, right? And I can tell you that a lot of my success in door to door sales was that making that sort of, that amazing first impression with my eye contact, my smile, how close I stood from the door or in someone's office, you know, what was my posture, was I leaning forward, was I leaning back, what was I even wearing? All those sort of things, when you're in person, they make just this incredible difference in your ability to get into rapport, in your ability to establish trust, and ultimately in your ability to get your point across and persuade someone to say yes. It's fucking crucial. So let's go through some, I wanna go through on the whiteboard here, a very basic aspect of this, okay? And this is something that you've heard many, many times before probably, it's this equation, they say about, you know, there's 100% of communication, right? How human beings communicate with each other, right? And the point that this typically is that they say, well, you probably think the words, you know, are the bulk of communication. And when you actually test this out in the laboratory and they've done so many studies, right? They typically get it to like where it's like about, you know, and I'm gonna round it up here, like, you know, 45% of the language, of actual communication is tonality, 45% is body language, body language, right? And only 10% are the words that you say. And that's in line with my terrible fucking handwriting, okay? So that, so the point of this is they try to tell you that this, you know, body language and tonality is just, you know, unconscious communication is power, okay, big fucking deal, right? Yes, I think we all know this at this point that, you know, the words matter 100%. I mean, it's not that words don't matter, it don't matter just 10%. They matter 100%. That's not what this equation means. What this equation means is that, while when you open up your mouth to speak, every word matters 100%. But we're constantly communicating without speaking. So when you're, even though you might not be speaking, you're still communicating with someone. You're communicating intention, thought, feeling, time, everything, you're communicating all these things and it happens mostly on an unconscious level. What that means is that they're not saying to themselves, hmm, this person is dressed this way. So that means he's trustworthy or he's a person of authority or an expert. It doesn't happen like that. It's simply, they look at you, they take it all in and it instantly bypasses the conscious mind. It goes straight to their gut, to their unconscious, right? And which is in your brain actually, right? And they make a decision based on a pattern that they formed. What I mean by that is the way our brains work is our brains form patterns, our unconscious mind. So your conscious mind has very limited processing power. Maybe, you know, a quality 3% of the world that you can actually take into it. So right now, as I talk to you, right, what am I really focusing on? My focus is my conscious mind. I'm focusing on the camera right in front of me, right? I see Anjai sitting right behind the camera, bowing to the left. I see the lights over here making the lighting purpose. So you can see me well, right? But what I'm not focusing on so much is the way the air feels on my skin or the background hum of the refrigerator over there. I'm not focusing on the fact that there's a light ray coming in, but now I am. See, there's all this stuff that's happening around me, even how the chair feels on my legs as I push, my gravity pushes me into the seat. I don't focus on that. Why? Because I can't. If I focused on all that shit, I'd go crazy. I don't like my conscious mind. I'm a smart guy, right? But my conscious mind has a limited amount of processing power. So what happens is my brain is a certain part of your brain called the reticular activating system that decides, that chooses, what your conscious mind focuses on. So at any given moment, my reticular activating system is saying, OK, it's important to focus on the camera. It's important to focus on what you're going to say next on your own train of thought. It's important to focus maybe on that these, I can't ignore certain stimuli. Like these lights are very bright. I can't ignore those, right? But it tells my conscious mind, don't worry about everything else. Don't worry. Ignore it all. Because it's irrelevant right now. To get the task done, the task at hand, this is what you should focus on. That's what your conscious mind, your reticular activating system does. So your conscious mind can't process everything. It's this much power, right? That being said, it's not like you lose all the other information. We would not be able to go through life like that. Because frankly, if a fucking like I played a practical joke on my staff last week, right? And on Halloween, I'd brought in this gigantic fucking 40 pound Python, like a 12 foot Python, phone was fucking up on a chair, right? I just had to, and Anjan, I went out through the day to a pet store. We rented this fucking gigantic Python, right? We snuck around the back and put it in the office. I had to slither up. Everyone's like, where the fuck was freaking out? What the fuck? It's like, right? Pretty funny. I think you can probably find it on Instagram. I don't know. Yeah. Oh, wow. It'll be on the next KPI. Yeah. OK. Next. Anyway, the point is that if all of a sudden, so yeah, I'm focusing all the stuff, but my unconscious mind sees everything, every little bit of my field, every noise I'm not focusing on, it's all going to this unconscious mind, which is constantly on watch to keep me safe. It regulates my heartbeat, my breathing, everything. Everything's being regulated. All my autonomic reflexes are being there. And if something should come into my field of view at any given moment, my unconscious mind will look at it and say, alert fucking snake, danger, sends this into my conscious mind. I'm like, what the fuck? And all of a sudden, I focus on that. That's how I'll, and this is really important. The reason I'm getting into this is important to understand, because what happens is when you're out there and selling and influencing, your prospect has only so much power in their conscious mind. So they focus on certain aspects of you. They focus on the words that you say, mostly. They focus on maybe some of that outstanding feature about you. They kind of got to go and try and sit on the space. There's something on the mole, the mole, right? Yeah, the mole, right? So they'll focus on one or two things, but everything else about you, how you're dressed, in the first second you walk in, yeah, they'll see that. But then, immediately, the brain does what's called habituates, meaning it sees something and it says, OK, no, it will eventually ignore it. Like, when you first walk in, you're getting a hot bath. It's fucking, you're like, oh, fucking so hot, right? Five minutes later, you think, oh, the water's just fine. That's not because the water cooled off. Let me agree. You got your body gets, you're probably saying, OK, it's safe. I'll delete out this information. It's not relevant. You get it? So what happens is the brain, the unconscious mind, in order to effectively manage you as you go through the world, throughout your life, it's taken all this extra information that you've deleted and it forms unconscious patterns, patterns of behavior. So when I woke up to a door, OK, and it's a strange door, and I've never seen the door before, I'm like, what the fuck? Wait a second. I've never seen it. I wonder, is it safe to go through this door? Is there a fucking, is there a different, the twilight zone outside? Is it going to be like a walk? It'll be a cliff outside? It could be wild animals waiting outside my door. But here's what happens. I go to a strange door and as I approach my unconscious mind, it's, ah, OK, pattern for doors in non-combat areas where there's not going to be fucking guns blazing, safe to go. And I immediately, I don't have to go through every door and stop and analyze and say, what's this? What's this little knob here? It's a door. It's a fucking door. Because my brain hasn't made a pattern for going through doors in non-combat zones. Promising this, drop me in Somalia or Iraq during a war, and I'm going to open every door and peek around what's causing your brain. Because I know I'm in a dangerous area. But the pattern for going through basic doors, right, that's already laid down in my mind. And it's going to be an automatic response. I'll walk through the door. It's why we can go through it without worrying about every crack in the sidewalk. You get it? Every new set of stairs we go through. I wonder if this leads to heaven, maybe? No, our brain knows that it's a pattern. Stairs lead to where? The next floor, right? It's been laid down over our entire lives, OK? The reason this is important is because when you're selling, when you're influencing, right, you need to be speaking to two minds at once. You're speaking to someone's logical mind, which is basically based on their conscious mind. It's a logical conscious mind and the unconscious mind, which we call the gut, gut feelings. And typically what happens is in the conscious mind, the influence occurs mostly through the words, we say, through the creation of logical cases, erratite logical cases through our words. Now, we then can apply certain tonalities to those words to give the words extra meaning. But those meanings that will pop up, if I say something to you like, if I say to you, hey, John, how you doing? You're like, I'm OK. I say, hey, John, how you doing today? And you're like, wow, you seem to really care. You heard those extra words, whether you knew it or not. You immediately applied extra meaning to my words because of the tonality I used, right? That happened on a gut level. You can't control it. As soon as I say my words a certain way, it registers in you, which is why those tonalities are so crucial when you're out influencing because you could add extra words on through your tonality. Well, guess what? The same thing happens with body language. And it's even more visceral with body language, especially when it comes to things like trust, respect, authority, those three things. Let me tell you something. One wrong body language move, and you're fucked. You really are. Not only can you not influence, but people will distrust you. I'll give you an example. Growing up, you might laugh at me, OK? But my son had a good friend. His name was Lucas, right? Lucas was a nice- I've got to say it. I hope he's not watching. No, it's OK. It's all good. But anyway, when Lucas was growing up, he would not make eye contact with you. He was like eight or nine years old and his friend Lucas. Lucas would come over and I'd say, hey, how you doing? He's like, I'm OK. I'm like, Lucas, where are you? Hey, Lucas, he wouldn't fucking make eye contact. And it made me distrust the kid. Every time I try, hey, Lucas, how you doing? I'm OK. Lucas, where are you? I'm like, Lucas, I look left. He wouldn't make eye contact, right? And because that, I distrusted him. Think about all the times that you've been in a sales setting where a salesman just said, he wouldn't look in the eye. There's something happening with that. And you just distrusted him. Go back even further. When you were in school, on the East Coast, I don't know if you had this a little something called cooties. Do you have cooties here in the West Coast? I'm going to have cooties. OK, so on the East Coast, you have things called cooties, right, when you were a kid, right? When you were really young. Oh, we're going to get a cootie shot with him because we're going to get some. And when you had someone who had cooties as a kid, right? Was it because of the words they said? No. It was how they looked. How they looked, honestly. I mean, let's call it Spader Spader. So that's what it was. It was how they looked, how they walked, how they spit when they talk. OK, you need this fucking spit guard, right? How they were discombobulated in some way. The hair was a mess. They were just unkempt in some way. And those kids were considered to be like, no, no, no, I'm going to cootie shot. Now, as we get old, we learn that's not nice, right? And we down-regulate our external response. But if you think for one fucking second that those patterns are not firmly laid down in you and in every prospect that you see, you're kidding yourself. We judge books by their covers. We do. Our parents did it. They said, don't judge a book by their cover. And they did it. And so do you. Our teachers said they did it. And we all do it. We judge books by their covers. It's a human thing. It's a survival mechanism that goes all the way back to the caveman days. And that's why I always say in those first four seconds of a conversation, if you're not perceived the right way, you're fucked. Now, we talk about being sharp as attack, enthusiastic as hell, and an expert in your field, a figure of authority. Sharp as attack, number one. I won't write it because you can't fucking understand it anyway. Enthusiastic as hell, an expert in your field. And then I say, well, why is that important? Because what that chunks up to is that you're a person worth listening to. And higher level still, because you can help them achieve their goals. You can help them get what they want. That's what it chunks up to. If you're just thinking about this for a second, let's just really slow this down here because now we're about to get specific of what body language inspires that and how do you avoid the pitfalls that cause that not to happen. Because let me tell you something. This is the keys to the kingdom of getting into quick rapport is body language and tonality when you're on the phone. We'll get to that later. But the point is, think about it for a second. What do you want to see in someone to make them to make this? What would you expect someone who's sharp to look like and act like and sound like? What should they look like? What should they sound like? Someone who's on the ball, a born problem solver. Someone who's not going to waste your time because they don't want to waste their own time. Why? Because they're also experts in their field. What is an expert in your field? I don't know what you sell. We can do some Q&A after we will. You can tell me what you sell. What do you sell? What should you look like? How should you dress? If you're a plumber, should you be wearing a three-piece suit to someone's door to fix their pipes? Probably not. They'll say, what the fuck? Is this the best guy's going to charge me an arm and a leg? I got to pay for a suit. They'll say, well, it doesn't make sense. We have something called the law of congruency. You need to dress the way it's congruent with your profession. How would an expert in your field dress? How would they look? How would they carry themselves? Why is that the case? Because it's about those patterns. We've been conditioned, since we're yay big, to essentially accept certain things as the norm and some things outside the norm. Some things were just visceral, like when a kid had the cooties, right, where you just knew it was off because it was outside the average. The same way they look at it, they've tested. My language has been tested, by the way, in laboratories. They do all these tests. They show people a face. And they can tell, based on the actual symmetry, how asymmetric someone's face is, the more asymmetric, the less trustworthy they're deemed. Seriously, the more symmetrical it is, our brain has been conditioned. And there are certain natural responses that we have where how someone looks, how someone carries themselves, how they dress, how they smell, how they move, what jewelry they wear, how they make eye contact, how they shake your hand, how far they stand from you, all these things. Do they stand directly in front of you? Do they stand up to the side? How do they move their hands when they're in front of you? What are their fingernails look? What are their shoes look like? And by the way, just so you know, guys, women are fucking brutal with this stuff. Women are 10 times more observant than men when it comes to body language. So if you're in a business, you're selling to a lot of women, you've gotta be really, really careful of every little piece of you. Guys are like dumb animals compared to women. And this is the part of the women, they just surpass us. They're incredibly observant, they see the whole picture, men are like, good, bad, hot, cold, when it comes to, compared to women, they're very, very observant when it comes to body language. So you have to be very cognizant of that fact and also the fact that there are different rules for men selling to men versus men selling to women and women selling to men, women selling to women. So let's dig in now and understand that if this is our goal, that in those first four seconds, you must be perceived as being sharp as a tack, enthusiastic as hell, an expert in your field, right? Because what that chunks up to is that you're a person worth listening to who can help them achieve their goals, right? Then we must then go to the next step. It's okay, well, the law of congruency states that, well, how should I dress? How should I act? How should I move? You're gonna do that in a way that's congruent with the profession that you're in. How people expect an expert in your field to act and move and dress. How they would expect an expert in your field to be enthusiastic, you understand? If you're an actor in Hollywood, it's very different than someone who's a plumber in the field or electrician or a dentist. Now, if a dentist comes out and he's all fucking sales, he's like, what the fuck? So you have to be congruent. A dentist is not gonna wear a suit. They wear a white dental smog. But imagine if it's filled with blood and pus or it's wrinkled and gross and their shoes are disgusting and they smile and their teeth are shitty. Good luck there, right? Or their hair is unkempt, or their fingernails are dirty, or they won't look you in the eye. You don't think dentistry is sales. I'll tell you a little story. Now, I'm not gonna tell it right now, but I have a great dent, one of the best ever. One of the best stories ever is my trip to the dentist. They, with the guy tried to sell me a fucking tooth and couldn't do it, all right? And then, you know, and he lost the $5,000 sale and I needed a tooth. And then I went through another dentist who knew exactly what to do and they got me a buy a $5,000 tooth in a second. So don't think when I say dentistry, I'm not kidding. Dentistry can be salespeople as well, we all do, okay? So my point is, is that I want you to be thinking out in context of this for you. And then we have the daily motivations to make sure that you're up on top of your game and keeping sharp. But these are intensives, right? So in context of the straight line, let's now get into actionable strategies for you starting on tomorrow or Monday, wherever you are in the world, right? That you can bring into the field, right? I never go face to face with another man. Once you do that, it immediately breaks rapport and what they say to themselves is nothing. They don't say a word, they say, something's wrong. So hands stay above, that's how you deal with sex differences, okay, next, this is a great one, invading the space bubble, okay? This is probably the most egregious violation of body language that you could ever make, and that are people who I call them close talkers, okay? Or space invaders, like, they're fucking space invaders, right, and typically along with space invading, they're usually spitters as well, you wanna get your fucking spit guard, they're fucking like an umbrella, they're fucking spitting on you as they talk, space invading is one of the biggest faux pas, so to speak, in sales, to get in someone's space bubble. Now, here's the deal, in the Western world, outside of certain parts of Asia, like Japan, China, okay, in the Western world, the space bubble was about two and a half to three feet, you do not want to get inside that bubble. Once you step inside that bubble, someone will feel uncomfortable, as soon as you go outside the bubble, they feel comfortable. Okay, now, if you're in Asia, people tend to stand a bit closer together there, and if you notice why they've even been to Asia, you might feel uncomfortable, and you're like, what the fuck, I'm that, and it's all cramped, and people will say it's cramped, but it's not that it's cramped, like, it's not like some, you know, whether they're zillion people, it's not like that so many people on the street there, cause the population is a lot, that's not why you feel cramped, you feel cramped, cause people will stand closer to you as you walk through the streets there, it's just out, they will go within two, now the bubble there is only about two feet, not that much, six inches, that's a lot in biolangers, so while it's really two and a half to three feet in the rest of the world, in certain parts of Asia, it's around two feet, so the reason you feel, people always say, oh my God, it's so crowded there, it's not that it's crowded, there's as many people anywhere, right? Yeah, there's a billion people, but there's a lot of space, it's not that, it's that they will get closer to you, cause it's their custom, and remember, this is about patterns of behavior, so what does that mean to you as a sales person, closing well, if you're trying to do business in Asia, if you stand, now remember, the space bubble also goes the other way, if you stand too far from someone, they feel put all, they don't trust you, like why won't this person come to the appropriate distance to these three feet, two and three quarters feet, okay? Next, okay, let's go through, what's called wrapping your package, all right? Meaning, how do you dress? You know, the basic premise that we have is like dressing for success, but I think that's a misconception, because, you know, it's really not about, I call dressing for congruency, dressing for what's appropriate, in other words, in some parts of the world, and in certain professions, you know, you dress one way, and other parts of the world, you dress another, so it's not like just, you know, cause dressing for success, almost like it makes you think, well, like a suit and tie almost, right? It makes you like dress for success, I gotta get, look really good, it's not that, okay? Dressing for success means dressing to close, dressing to close sales, dressing to influence, dressing to persuade, and that means you need to dress, how an expert is expected to dress in your field. Let me give you an example. I go back to the plumber again, right? So with the plumber, what the fuck was that? Okay, okay, it's fine. So with the plumber, right? So how should a plumber, an electrician, how should someone like more of a laborer type, right? That's doing actual manual labor, come when we go to someone's house, how do you dress? How should you dress? Well, you don't wanna dress in a nice suit and fancy clothes, cause they'll say, what the fuck, is he trying to get, what's going on here? He's like, he doesn't seem like he's in the profession, it's not right, it's just, it makes people, anything that makes someone take pause is not good when it comes to by language, right? Okay, and look, we have this cat just walked in here, we have a cat, we have a cat that loves us, he just walks around the frickin' neighborhood here, and we fed him once and now he will never leave, okay? Anyway, let him hang out here, okay? So you have, cause how do you dress a plumber? The way a plumber should dress, nice, crisp shirt, work shirt, right? Patch, over here over the chest, his name on the patch, the company's name above that, nice, okay, clean work shoes, not dirty and disgusting, belt with his tools on there or whatever, holding a clipboard in his hand when he comes in your door, right, or with a thing holding a clipboard, how's that, right? That's, you say, well, okay, this guy looks, he's gonna come in there and fix my pipes, do a nice, clean job, he'll do a great job, right? Well, what if the same guy came in and he had his pants sagging down to his ass cracks, he had plumbers butt, he had a disgusting shirt with paint splatter on it, and he himself was unkempt and dirty. What would you think of that guy? Well, you might not throw him out of your house if you need your pipes fixed, right? But, you know, in the world of plumbing, there's massive sales opportunities. In other words, the plumbing world is typically based on emergency calls. You don't like call plumbing, hey, you wanna just come in and do a normal check here? Make sure my pipes are clean? Double intangible, right? And the point is, is that, you know, you don't, I couldn't help that, right? You don't call a plumbing, make sure your pipes are clean. You go to like fucking four and a half, okay? Anyway, that's true, right? Anyway, the point is, is that the world of plumbing electricians, right, laborers, not as much as they construct, mostly plumbing, electricity, right? It's a lot of emergency calls. Might have a flood in the house, things aren't working, and you call up an emergency number, they send over the plumber or the electrician, and he comes in and he fixes something, right? Well, guess what? You know, I was hired by a plumbing company a bunch of years back, and they wanted me to help increase their sales, right? So we came up with a program for them, where essentially a plumber would come in, they'd get an emergency call, and a plumber would come in, and rather than just going in and fixing the thing and leaving, right, he'd say, oh, you know, as long as we want to do a free, you know, health check of your entire pipes and everything, right? And you're having to get, say, yeah, of course, it's free, right, and the guy would come in, and variably, of course, right? If you check someone's pipes around the house, they'll think, you know, the sinks in the bath, you're gonna find problems, corrosion, leaks, things that are about to break, right? And what they found is about 80% of the time, they're able to turn an $80 emergency call, where they actually lose money on the call, and turn it to a $1,000 sale, by doing this free survey around the house, right? What happened was, you found out pretty quickly, that some of the plumbers were really good at it, the ones that actually knew how to sell would do great, and those that weren't so good at it, would have, would struggle, right? And what it came down to, was the ones that knew how to sell would come in, they would look good, they would act, they knew all the secrets to body language, basic persuasion, right? So I started retraining them, and that worked as well, too, we trained people, and had them wear their nice clothing, right? You know, the right, you know, patch, and I think, you know, clean cut, show them how to make eye contact, and that worked. Worked so well, in fact, that the company almost blew itself up, because what happened was, is every single job turned into like a long, so a one hour emergency call, turned into like a three day job always, right? So they had no more plumbers out there that answered our emergency calls, they started getting calls that couldn't answer, sorry, your gym is backed up now, but they got back, they couldn't grow their business anymore. So we had to restructure it, so that we would actually do the health check, and say, you know what, we'll fix the emergency now, and we can actually do the whole, we can actually do the whole, all the work now, or if you let us come back on a scheduled call next week, we'll give you a 30% discount, and say, oh, I'll take the discount, and you start scheduling in all these extra jobs you picked up, and having, so what we did is then, we took the ones that were really natural at sales, and were doing well, we made those the ones that would do your emergency calls. So the emergency calls were handled by someone who actually was a great sales person, and they would do the survey, and then sell them on a much bigger job, right? And then for the actual work that came with these jobs, bigger jobs, you'd send the guys that really weren't that great at selling, and they'd actually do the manual labor, you get it? So don't, when I say plumbers, that's a sales, you can really make sales as a plumber, any contractor, if you know how to do it. Key is, is that what we found, the big thing was, is that when they showed up with the clipboard, the name patch, clean cut, tool belt, company hat, with the name on it, sales spiked, and they had a lot less problem actually closing on those bigger jobs. Before that training, you had some that were naturally good and a lot that weren't, okay? So the point is, for a plumber or any manual laborer, it's not about a suit and tie, it's about dressing the way you expect someone to dress. Okay, now they say this is aggressive. Like when Donald Trump goes, he goes, this, how you doing? They say it's not aggressive. It's more trying to display warmth and feeling. Like more rapport. So you're great. Hey buddy, how you doing here? How do you feel? Very comfortable. Comfortable, right? So I, if I wanna show some extra warmth, it's how you doing, nice to meet you. Put it like this, on the patio, not too much, it's enough to know. Not wanting to feel like, hey, how you doing? I'm screaming. You ever had that with someone else to massage you? You're like, what the fuck? I had friends of guys that would do it. They would fucking rub you, fucking give you a massage. You're like, oh, nice to meet you, right? Okay, so again, that's called your basic cooperated handshake. Then you have the aggressive, which is how you doing? Like when they're like, damn Glent. And you're like, what the fuck does that guy have to prove? And it turns people off. And then you have the worst one of all, which is the limp, how you doing? Nice to, you know, the, and by the way, thanks. That's that, by the way, interestingly enough, that last one that like that limp, that's almost your ultimate power handshake. We were like, it's like the one saying, I don't give a shit about you. Like I'm so above you. I'm not even gonna expend the energy it takes to shake your fucking hand. Believe it or not, that's what that means. So remember, for me, to play it safe, I always use the basic cooperators handshake, which is straight up, not like this, not like that. So let me finish up here. Active listening. Active listening is the crucial, it's really something that I, it's a phrase I invented, by the way, okay? I've never heard of it before. Active listening is how you essentially listen, you ask questions, and then when you listen to someone respond, you don't just listen, you give them feedback in the forms, uh-huh, mm-hmm, mm-hmm, yep, mm-hmm, mm-hmm, mm-hmm, mm-hmm. aspect that's called pacing and leading, of how you use, the whole thing about, you know, someone's closed, closed body language, you wanna have someone open, now, I'm gonna close with this thought, and we'll go to our Q and A, okay, is that sometimes if someone sits like this, it's not cause they're closed, they're fucking cold, it's not the way someone, no, obviously, rather have someone like this, than like this, but it's not the, it's much more subtle than that, like that's like the lowest level of body language, the point is, is that you can, and we'll close with this thought here, is that you can change someone else's body language by using your own body language, something called pacing and leading, which I'll get into next with tonality, when we do tonality next, but pacing and leading basically means that you can, you know, you enter someone's role there, so if someone is sitting like this, then you wanna basically adopt the same physiologies then, sit, and then you sit there, and then you open up yourself, and they'll follow you, so you enter someone, so if someone's like this, and you want it to be more open, then you actually start, and you sit like this, like shoulder hearts, I can't even do it, right? So you sit like this, and then you, uh-huh, uh-huh, yep, and then if you open up, okay, then they will tend to open up, remember, you wanna adopt someone else's physiology as much as possible, they're sitting a certain way, then you sit a certain way, okay, but eventually you wanna have them follow you, so if you enter their role where they are, then you pace them, you say, you stay that way, then you slowly move in the direction you want them to go, that's the key, but don't get so caught, I'm not a big believer in this whole closed open yet, it's better to be open, but sometimes people just sit this way, my wife does, she's always sit like this, but she's cold, she's not closed, okay, so don't just take that one to the bank, it's like the first thing you see, we built for the bio-library, right?
